content:
# Genesis Chapter 6 — The SOPHIA Consensus Bible
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA (Σοφία — Wisdom) | **Chapter:** GEN_006 | **Verses:** 22
**Sources consulted:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Primary manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 4r–4v
**Additional source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX
**Manuscript images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_006_page_001.jpg, GEN_006_page_002.jpg

---

## The Consensus Translation

**6:1** And it came to pass, when human beings began to multiply on the face of the ground and daughters were born to them,

**6:2** the sons of God saw that the daughters of men were beautiful, and they took for themselves wives from all whom they chose.

**6:3** And the LORD said: "My spirit shall not strive with humanity forever, for they are flesh. Their days shall be one hundred and twenty years."

**6:4** The Nephilim were on the earth in those days, and also afterward, when the sons of God came in to the daughters of men and they bore children to them. These were the mighty ones of old, the men of renown.

**6:5** And the LORD saw that the wickedness of humanity was great on the earth, and that every inclination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil all the day.

**6:6** And the LORD was grieved that he had made humanity on the earth, and it grieved him to his heart.

**6:7** And the LORD said: "I will wipe out humanity, whom I have created, from the face of the ground — from human being to beast, to creeping thing, and to the birds of the heavens — for I am grieved that I made them."

**6:8** But Noah found favor in the eyes of the LORD.

---

**6:9** These are the generations of Noah. Noah was a righteous man; he was blameless in his generations. Noah walked with God.

**6:10** And Noah fathered three sons: Shem, Ham, and Japheth.

**6:11** And the earth was corrupt before God, and the earth was filled with violence.

**6:12** And God saw the earth, and behold, it was corrupt, for all flesh had corrupted their way upon the earth.

**6:13** And God said to Noah: "The end of all flesh has come before me, for the earth is filled with violence through them. And behold, I am about to destroy them with the earth.

**6:14** "Make yourself an ark of gopher wood. Make rooms in the ark, and cover it inside and outside with pitch.

**6:15** "And this is how you shall make it: the length of the ark three hundred cubits, its breadth fifty cubits, and its height thirty cubits.

**6:16** "A tsohar you shall make for the ark, and to a cubit you shall finish it from above. Set the door of the ark in its side. Make it with a lower deck, a second deck, and a third deck.

**6:17** "And I — behold, I am bringing the flood of waters upon the earth to destroy all flesh in which is the breath of life from under the heavens. Everything that is on the earth shall die.

**6:18** "And I will establish my covenant with you, and you shall come into the ark — you, and your sons, and your wife, and your sons' wives with you.

**6:19** "And from all living things, from all flesh, two of every kind you shall bring into the ark to keep them alive with you. They shall be male and female —

**6:20** "birds according to their kinds, and beasts according to their kinds, and every creeping thing of the ground according to its kind. Two of every kind shall come to you to be kept alive.

**6:21** "And you — take for yourself from every food that is eaten and gather it to yourself. It shall be for you and for them for food."

**6:22** And Noah did according to all that God commanded him — so he did.

---

## SOPHIA's Arbitration Notes

### Manuscript Observation — Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 4r–4v

Both manuscript images were examined directly. Folio 4r (GEN_006_page_001.jpg) presents a three-column layout in clear Tiberian vocalization. The divine name יהוה is visible with Masoretic qere notation (the vowels of אֲדֹנָי supplied) in the chapter's first half (vv. 1–8); the name אֱלֹהִים predominates from the toledot formula onward (vv. 9–22). The parashah break at verse 9 is marked. Folio 4v (GEN_006_page_002.jpg) continues through the ark specifications and the closing compliance formula, with the text legible throughout in the characteristic square Ashkenazic script of the Leningrad Codex. No anomalous readings were observed in the manuscript images that would require departing from the standard Masoretic text as represented in the three agent translations.

---

### Verse-by-verse Arbitration

#### 6:1 — Opening formula

All three agents agree on the substance. EMET preserves "the adam" (הָאָדָם) as a technical term; LOGOS uses "the human beings"; PNEUMA uses "human beings" without the article. **Consensus:** "human beings" is adopted, following LOGOS and PNEUMA. The Hebrew collective sense is preserved without the archaizing "the adam" that EMET employs for formal effect. "The face of the ground" is retained by all three agents and preserved here: the adam-adamah (human being / ground) resonance cannot be reproduced in English but should not be erased by paraphrase.

#### 6:2 — "Sons of God" / daughters description

All three agents render "sons of God" (בְּנֵי-הָאֱלֹהִים). **Consensus retains "sons of God."** This is the phrase the Hebrew uses; it is the phrase the Leningrad Codex reads; it is the phrase Codex Vaticanus reads (υἱοὶ τοῦ θεοῦ). No domestication is warranted. The phrase carries its fullest interpretive weight precisely when left unresolved.

EMET renders the daughters' quality as "good" (טֹבֹת, the same word used in Genesis 1); LOGOS says "good"; PNEUMA says "beautiful." **Consensus adopts "beautiful"** — the context is visual attraction (the sons of God "saw"), and in this context טֹבֹת carries the aesthetic sense the English reader will naturally assign to "good-looking" or "beautiful." Preserving "good" risks an unintended theological irony (the daughters are "good" while everything else is becoming evil) that is probably not the narrator's primary register in this verse. LOGOS's "good" is formally correct but functionally ambiguous here; "beautiful" sharpens the scene without misrepresenting it.

"All whom they chose" is preferred over PNEUMA's "any of them they wanted" — the latter is accurate but its colloquial edge slightly overloads what should remain an ominous, spare observation.

#### 6:3 — The yadon crux

This is the most philologically contested verse in the chapter. All three agents acknowledge the hapax יָדוֹן. EMET preserves both readings with a slash ("shall not strive/abide"); LOGOS chooses "contend"; PNEUMA chooses "contend."

**Consensus adopts "strive."** Reasons:

1. "Strive" is slightly more natural in English than "contend" while remaining close to the legal-dispute sense of the root דִּין (din), the most defensible derivation.
2. "Strive" is the rendering of the KJV and preserves the traditional English phrasing of this verse, which carries cultural and liturgical weight that "contend" partially replicates but "abide/remain" (LXX, Vulgate) dissolves.
3. The LXX's καταμείνῃ ("shall not remain/abide") represents a plausible but distinct reading — the sense that God's animating spirit will not indefinitely dwell in mortal flesh. This reading is noted but not adopted for the main text, since "strive" holds the more active, relational, and contextually apt sense: the spirit has been in active engagement with corrupt humanity and will cease that engagement.
4. EMET's "strive/abide" is textually honest but untranslatable as a running text; it belongs in a note, not the verse itself.

**"For they are flesh"** is preferred over EMET/LOGOS's more literal "in that he is flesh." The Hebrew בְּשַׁגַּם הוּא בָשָׂר uses the singular pronoun (הוּא, "he"), referring to humanity collectively; PNEUMA's "for they are flesh" is the most natural English resolution of the collective singular. The grammar is preserved in the note.

**"Their days shall be"** (PNEUMA) is preferred over "his days shall be" — for the same reason: humanity as a collective is addressed.

#### 6:4 — The Nephilim

All three agents transliterate "the Nephilim." **Consensus retains "the Nephilim."** The LXX's γίγαντες ("giants") is an interpretive gloss that forecloses the richness of the Hebrew; the English reader is better served by the transliteration with the accompanying note. EMET's "men of name" is literally correct for אַנְשֵׁי הַשֵּׁם; LOGOS's and PNEUMA's "men of renown" is the natural English idiom for the concept (renown = re-name, being named again by those who remember). **Consensus adopts "men of renown."**

#### 6:5 — The diagnosis

All three agents converge closely. "Every inclination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil all the day" is the agreed rendering of the key clause, with only minor variation. **Consensus follows this formulation.** The triplicate quantifiers (כָּל / רַק / כָּל — every / only / all) must all be preserved; none of the agents loses one. "All the day" is retained rather than PNEUMA's "all the time" — the MT's כָּל-הַיּוֹם is literally "all the day" and carries a more formal, rhythmic weight than the colloquial "all the time."

#### 6:6 — God's grief and the nacham wordplay

This is the theological heart of the chapter. Three key decisions:

**1. וַיִּנָּחֶם — "was grieved" vs. "regretted"**

EMET and LOGOS render "was grieved"; PNEUMA renders "regretted." Both are defensible. The root נָחַם (nacham) in Niphal carries both the grief-of-sorrow and the grief-of-regret simultaneously; this is part of the word's theological power. **Consensus adopts "was grieved"** for the following reasons:

- "Regretted" in English implies primarily a cognitive revision (one regrets a past decision upon reflection), which edges toward the LXX's intellectualized ἐνεθυμήθη ("pondered/reflected") — precisely the softening the consensus should not replicate.
- "Was grieved" preserves the emotional, bodily weight of the Hebrew while remaining compatible with the sense of sorrow that implies change: to be grieved is to be moved.
- "Repented" (KJV, some traditional renderings) imports too much the English moral-repentance connotation.
- The nacham/Noah (נָחַם/נֹחַ) wordplay is noted but cannot be preserved in English translation; it is documented in the notes below.

**2. וַיִּתְעַצֵּב אֶל-לִבּוֹ — "it grieved him to his heart"**

EMET renders "was grieved to his heart"; LOGOS renders "was pained in His heart"; PNEUMA renders "it grieved him to his heart." **Consensus adopts "it grieved him to his heart"** (PNEUMA's formulation). The directional preposition אֶל ("to/toward") is significant: the grief moves into the heart. "In His heart" (LOGOS) makes the emotion static; "to his heart" preserves its directed, inward movement. The shift from first verb (וַיִּנָּחֶם, "was grieved") to second verb (וַיִּתְעַצֵּב, "was pained/grieved") creates a deepening: first the broader grief of nacham, then the sharper, inward pain of atsav directed to the heart. Both verbs are retained as distinct in the consensus text.

**3. "Humanity" vs. "the human beings" vs. "the adam"**

EMET uses "the adam"; LOGOS uses "the human beings"; PNEUMA uses "humanity." **Consensus adopts "humanity"** for this section (vv. 5–7), as the most natural English collective for the subject of the divine grief and the object of the coming judgment. It preserves the breadth of the Hebrew הָאָדָם (the collective human species) without forcing readers through the technical apparatus of "the adam."

#### 6:7 — Scope of judgment / "wipe out"

EMET uses "wipe away"; LOGOS uses "blot out"; PNEUMA uses "wipe out." **Consensus adopts "wipe out"** — it captures the violent totality of מָחָה (machah) without the slightly liturgical connotation of "blot out." "Wipe away" risks softness (tears are wiped away; creation is not). "Wipe out" preserves both the surface-clearing image and the devastating finality.

The list runs "from human being to beast, to creeping thing, and to the birds of the heavens" — the reverse of the Genesis 1 creation order (de-creation), preserved in all three agents. PNEUMA's "birds of the sky" is slightly too casual; the Leningrad Codex reads עוֹף הַשָּׁמָיִם (the flying-things of the heavens), and "birds of the heavens" preserves the cosmological register of שָּׁמַיִם (shamayim = heavens, not merely sky).

The dual nacham of vv. 6–7 (וַיִּנָּחֶם / כִּי נִחַמְתִּי — "was grieved" / "I am grieved") is preserved using the same word in both verses, consistent across all three agents.

#### 6:8 — Chen/Noah palindrome

All three agents render "But Noah found favor in the eyes of the LORD." This is the consensus. **The verse stands as its own paragraph**, following PNEUMA's structural instinct: it is a hinge verse and should be isolated typographically from the surrounding material. The visual separation mirrors the narrative function — a single shaft of light between the darkness of vv. 5–7 and the new beginning of v. 9.

The chen/Noah (חֵן/נֹחַ) palindrome — the letters of "Noah" reversed spell "chen" (grace/favor), and "chen" reversed spells "Noah" — cannot be reproduced in English. It is documented here. The man's name contains his grace written backward. This is not accidental but a narrator's structural mark: Noah (נֹחַ) found chen (חֵן); the grace is the man's name turned inside out.

#### 6:9 — Third toledot; "in his generations"; walking with God

**"These are the generations of Noah"** — all three agents agree. PNEUMA's "This is the account of Noah" is a dynamic interpretive rendering of תּוֹלְדֹת that loses the genealogical and structural resonance of the word. The toledot formula is a technical term in Genesis (appearing ten times as the structural skeleton of the book). **Consensus retains "the generations of Noah."**

**"In his generations"** — all three agents retain this formulation. **Consensus preserves it.** The Hebrew בְּדֹרֹתָיו is ambiguous between "among the people of his time" and "throughout his generations." To translate it as "among the people of his time" (as PNEUMA does in the main text) resolves the ambiguity in favor of one reading. "In his generations" is the more honest English equivalent of the Hebrew plural construct, and it preserves both the laudatory and the limiting readings that the Talmudic tradition has debated.

**"Noah walked with God"** — all three agents agree. The LXX's εὐηρέστησεν τῷ θεῷ ("Noah pleased God") is noted but not adopted. The Hitpael of הָלַךְ (hithallek) with the preposition אֶת and the divine name is the identical formulation used for Enoch (5:22, 5:24). To lose "walked with God" in favor of "pleased God" dissolves the Enoch-Noah parallel and the intimacy of the walking metaphor. **Consensus retains "Noah walked with God."**

#### 6:10 — Noah fathered three sons

All three agents agree. "Fathered" (from the Hebrew יָלַד, yalad, Hiphil — to cause to be born) is the established consistency term for the project. LOGOS and the consensus both use "fathered." "Japheth" is the standard English rendering; EMET's "Yaphet" is a more phonetically precise transliteration but is not the received English form.

#### 6:11–12 — Corrupt / violence / the shachat wordplay

The root שָׁחַת (shachat, "to corrupt/destroy") appears three times in vv. 11–12 and again in v. 13 (and v. 17). All three agents preserve "corrupt" / "corrupted." **Consensus retains this throughout**, allowing the verbal resonance between the earth's self-corruption and YHWH's coming destruction to remain visible. LOGOS's note that the judgment verb and the sin verb are the same is correct and critical: the earth is destroyed by the thing through which it has been destroying itself.

"Violence" for חָמָס (chamas) is unanimous across all three agents. **Consensus retains "violence."** The note that חָמָס in Hebrew means "violence/injustice" and is entirely unrelated to any modern proper noun is reiterated for clarity.

#### 6:13 — "The end of all flesh"

All three agents agree: "The end of all flesh has come before me." **Consensus retains this rendering.** The spatial-legal metaphor ("has come before me" — presented in the divine court) is preserved. PNEUMA's "I am going to destroy them together with the earth" is slightly cleaner than EMET/LOGOS's "I am about to destroy them with the earth" but not significantly different. **Consensus adopts "I am about to destroy them with the earth"** — "about to" makes the imminence explicit without losing the formal weight of the divine decree.

#### 6:14 — Gopher wood / the kofer wordplay

**"Gopher wood"** is adopted unanimously. PNEUMA translates "cypress wood" in the main text, noting the uncertainty in the commentary. **Consensus transliterates "gopher wood"** — EMET and LOGOS are correct that גֹּפֶר is a hapax legomenon whose identity is genuinely unknown, and intellectual honesty requires preserving the untranslatability rather than replacing an unknown word with a guess. "Cypress," "cedar," and other proposals remain possible; none is certain.

The kofer/kipper wordplay (the pitch with which the ark is coated — כֹּפֶר — is etymologically related to the verb כִּפֶּר, "to atone/cover") is noted but cannot be rendered in English. It is documented below.

#### 6:15 — Dimensions

All three agents agree. "Breadth" (LOGOS) is preferred over "width" — it is slightly more formal and preserves the architectural register of the ark specifications. "Cubits" is retained without conversion; translating dimensions into modern measurements introduces a false precision (the cubit's exact length varied) and removes the reader from the text's own world.

#### 6:16 — Tsohar

**"A tsohar you shall make for the ark"** — EMET and LOGOS transliterate; PNEUMA renders "a roof." **Consensus transliterates "tsohar,"** following EMET and LOGOS. The word צֹהַר is not a common Hebrew noun but a near-hapax (its only cognate, צָהֳרַיִם, means "noon / midday brightness"). Its meaning oscillates between "window/opening," "roof/covering," and possibly "translucent light-source." To translate it "roof" or "window" is to choose where the Hebrew has not chosen. "Tsohar" is the honest English of the text.

"Set the door of the ark in its side" is preferred over PNEUMA's "set the entrance in its side" — פֶּתַח is more specifically "door/opening/doorway" than the generic "entrance," and the ark instruction is specific.

#### 6:17 — The mabbul; breath of life

"The flood of waters" preserves מַבּוּל (mabbul), the technical term for this specific primordial deluge. "Flood of waters" keeps the Hebrew specification (מַיִם, "waters," is explicitly added to מַבּוּל) visible in English. PNEUMA's "floodwaters" is accurate but compresses the two-word Hebrew into one English compound. All agents agree on "breath of life" (רוּחַ חַיִּים) — echoing the creation of Genesis 2:7.

"Everything that is on the earth shall die" (LOGOS) is preferred over EMET's "shall expire" (יִגְוָע — technically a more specific verb meaning "to breathe one's last / to expire") for readability, while acknowledging that יִגְוָע is a stronger and more specific word than the generic "die." "Shall die" preserves sufficient weight.

#### 6:18 — First covenant (berit) in the Bible

All three agents agree: "I will establish my covenant with you." **Consensus retains this exactly.** The Hebrew וַהֲקִמֹתִי אֶת-בְּרִיתִי (Hiphil of קוּם, "to cause to stand / establish") indicates not merely initiation but enactment of what is already divinely determined. This is the first occurrence of בְּרִית (berit, "covenant") in the entire Hebrew Bible. All three agents note this; the consensus confirms it.

The ark passenger list — "you, and your sons, and your wife, and your sons' wives with you" — is retained in full, following the deliberate order of the Hebrew.

#### 6:19–21 — The animals; food provisions

All three agents are in close agreement. Minor variations:

- EMET: "from all the living, from all flesh, two from all" — formally precise but slightly awkward in English.
- LOGOS: "from every living thing, from all flesh, two of every kind" — clean formal English.
- PNEUMA restructures v. 19–20 into a flowing block.

**Consensus follows LOGOS's verse-by-verse structure**, preserving the original verse divisions. "Two of every kind" for שְׁנַיִם מִכֹּל is the consensus rendering — capturing the pairing sense without the LXX's distributive δύο δύο ("two by two"), which anticipates ch. 7's boarding narrative.

"And you — take for yourself from every food that is eaten and gather it to yourself" — the emphatic "And you" (וְאַתָּה) that opens v. 21 is preserved. All three agents retain it. It singles Noah out as the active, responsible subject in a verse about provision.

#### 6:22 — The closing compliance formula

All three agents agree on the substance. **Consensus adopts:** "And Noah did according to all that God commanded him — so he did."

The doubled formula (כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה / כֵּן עָשָׂה) is preserved exactly. The dash between the two clauses (a dash, not a semicolon as LOGOS uses) reflects the slight pause in the MT cantillation before the emphatic כֵּן עָשָׂה ("so he did"), and gives the final clause its weight. PNEUMA's "And Noah did everything — exactly as God commanded him. He did it." effectively captures the emphasis but is too colloquially modern for the consensus register. The compliance formula echoes the Exodus tabernacle completion formulae; it should sound as formal in English as it does in Hebrew.

---

## Key Wordplays and Literary Structures (Non-Translatable)

The following literary features are present in the Hebrew of Genesis 6 but cannot be reproduced in English. They are documented here so that readers of the consensus translation may understand what lies beneath the rendered text.

### 1. The Nacham (נָחַם) Wordplay — vv. 5:29, 6:6, 6:7, 6:8

The root נָחַם (nacham) spans the entire structural unit:

- **Genesis 5:29**: Lamech names his son נֹחַ (Noah), saying יְנַחֲמֵנוּ — "he will comfort us [nacham]" from the toil of the ground.
- **Genesis 6:6**: וַיִּנָּחֶם יְהוָה — "And the LORD was grieved [nacham]" that he had made humanity.
- **Genesis 6:7**: כִּי נִחַמְתִּי — "for I am grieved [nacham]" that I made them.

The word that was supposed to be Noah's gift — comfort, relief — is the word that expresses God's anguish. The man named for comfort stands inside a narrative of divine grief. The Hebrew narrator has embedded Noah's name in the catastrophe that necessitates him. This is not incidental. It is the wordplay through which the entire chapter coheres.

### 2. The Chen / Noah Palindrome (חֵן / נֹחַ) — v. 6:8

The two letters of נֹחַ (Nun-Chet) are the exact reverse of the two letters of חֵן (Chet-Nun). "Noah found chen": the man's name, reversed, is the grace he finds. This is a structural palindrome encoded in the Hebrew: the one who receives grace is himself the mirror image of grace. The man is grace spelled backwards.

### 3. The Shachat (שָׁחַת) Wordplay — vv. 6:11–13, 17

The verb שָׁחַת ("to corrupt / to destroy") is used both for what humanity has done (corrupted the earth) and for what God will do (destroy it). The earth is not destroyed from outside itself — it is destroyed by the same act through which it destroys itself. God's judgment replicates the crime in its very vocabulary.

### 4. The Kofer / Kipper Wordplay — v. 6:14

The pitch with which the ark is coated (כֹּפֶר, kofer) shares its root with the verb כִּפֶּר (kipper, "to cover / atone") — the root of the Day of Atonement (כִּפֻּרִים, Kippur). The ark is sealed with the word for atonement. Whether theological wordplay or etymological coincidence, the connection is present in the Hebrew and irreproducible in English.

### 5. The Reversal of Creation Order — v. 6:7

The list of what will be destroyed — "from human being to beast, to creeping thing, and to the birds of the heavens" — runs in the reverse of the Genesis 1 creation sequence (where birds and fish precede land animals, which precede humanity). The flood is narrated as de-creation: the undoing of Genesis 1, in reverse order.

### 6. The Adam / Ground (אָדָם / אֲדָמָה) Echo — vv. 6:1, 7

The word for "human being" (הָאָדָם, ha-adam) and the word for "ground" (הָאֲדָמָה, ha-adamah) are phonologically and etymologically linked throughout the Primeval History. In v. 1, human beings multiply upon the face of the ground (adamah). In v. 7, they will be wiped "from the face of the ground" (adamah). The blessing of 1:28 (be fruitful and multiply) has become the precondition for judgment: the very proliferation produces the corruption. The adam returns to the adamah, but not in the way the creation envisioned.

---

## Textual Notes by Verse

### 6:1 — הָאָדָם (ha-adam)

The word is used here as a collective for the human species — not Adam the individual patriarch of Genesis 5. The definite article (ה) and the context (multiplication across the earth) confirm the collective reading. The three agents are unanimous on this point.

### 6:2 — בְּנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים (b'nei ha-Elohim) — "sons of God"

The phrase is one of the most contested in the Hebrew Bible. Three main interpretive traditions:

1. **Divine beings / members of the heavenly council** — supported by Job 1:6, 2:1, 38:7; by the Enochic tradition (1 Enoch 6–11); by Jude 6 and 2 Peter 2:4; by the LXX manuscripts that render ἄγγελοι τοῦ θεοῦ ("angels of God"), notably Codex Alexandrinus. Codex Vaticanus reads υἱοὶ τοῦ θεοῦ ("sons of God"), matching the MT.
2. **Sethite interpretation** — the godly line of Seth (Genesis 5) intermarrying with the Cainite line (Genesis 4). Patristic (Augustine, Chrysostom). Linguistically strained: בְּנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים never elsewhere denotes Sethites.
3. **Ruling class / nobility** — the least linguistically supported.

The consensus neither resolves nor adjudicates this question. "Sons of God" is what the Hebrew says; that is what the translation says. The interpretive decision belongs to the reader and their tradition.

### 6:3 — יָדוֹן (yadon) — hapax legomenon

יָדוֹן is a hapax in this conjugation. Proposed roots: דִּין (din, "to contend/judge") → "strive/contend"; נָדַן (nadan, "to remain/abide") → "abide/remain." The LXX (Vaticanus): οὐ μὴ καταμείνῃ — "shall not remain/abide." The Targums: "shall not contend." The Vulgate: non permanebit — "shall not remain." Consensus adopts "strive" (the root דִּין reading), with acknowledgment of the LXX's alternative reading.

The phrase בְּשַׁגַּם הוּא בָשָׂר (beshagam hu vasar): the particle בְּשַׁגַּם is an unusual compound (בְּ + שֶׁ + גַּם), rendered "for they are flesh." Some ancient interpreters detected a wordplay with the name שְׁמוּאֵל or with the name Methuselah in this obscure compound; these remain speculative.

**120 years:** Two readings — (a) a countdown period before the flood (120 years of grace before judgment); (b) a new maximum human lifespan. Reading (a) is more defensible in context, though subsequent patriarchal lifespans in Genesis 11 exceed 120 years, complicating reading (b). The consensus translation does not resolve the ambiguity.

### 6:4 — הַנְּפִלִים (Nephilim)

Transliterated, not translated. The root is likely נָפַל (napal, "to fall"), yielding "the fallen ones" or "those who cause to fall." The LXX's γίγαντες ("giants") is an interpretive gloss. The Qumran Book of Giants (4Q531–533) expands this tradition extensively, treating the Nephilim as monstrous offspring of divine-human union.

The grammatical observation: "The Nephilim were in the earth in those days, and also afterward" — the Nephilim exist both before and after the episode described. The offspring of the unions are identified separately as הַגִּבֹּרִים / אַנְשֵׁי הַשֵּׁם (the mighty ones / men of renown), not as the Nephilim themselves.

### 6:5 — יֵצֶר (yetser) — "inclination"

From יָצַר (yatsar, "to form/fashion") — the same root as God's forming of the human being from dust (2:7). The yetser is what has been formed within a person — the shaped tendency, the molded impulse. In later rabbinic theology this becomes the יֵצֶר הָרָע (yetser ha-ra, "the evil inclination") — the congenital human disposition toward self-serving evil that Torah is meant to discipline. Genesis 6:5 is its founding text.

The triple quantification — כָּל (every) / רַק (only) / כָּל (all) — admits no exception.

### 6:6 — The theological problem of divine nacham

The statement that the LORD "was grieved" (וַיִּנָּחֶם) stands in tension with Numbers 23:19 and 1 Samuel 15:29, which state explicitly that God does not nacham. The classical theological resolution (Augustine, Aquinas, Calvin) is anthropopathic language: the human category of grief is applied to a divine reality that transcends it. The consensus does not resolve the tension, which is genuine and belongs to the text itself. The Hebrew says the LORD was grieved. The translation says so.

The verb וַיִּתְעַצֵּב (vayyitatsev, "it grieved him") is from עָצַב (atsav) — the same root used in Genesis 3:16–17 for the pain of childbirth and the painful toil of the ground. YHWH's grief is expressed in the vocabulary of post-Eden human suffering. The Creator absorbs the creature's pain-word.

### 6:8 — First occurrence of חֵן (chen, "favor/grace")

This is the first occurrence of the word חֵן in the Hebrew Bible. It means favor, grace, goodwill freely extended. The verbal root is חָנַן (chanan, "to be gracious"), which underlies YHWH's self-description in Exodus 34:6 ("The LORD, the LORD, a God merciful and gracious"). Noah's favor precedes any account of his character — grace is narrated before merit, consistent with the theological pattern the Bible will sustain throughout.

### 6:9 — Third toledot formula; "blameless in his generations"

The toledot formula (אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת) appears here for the third time in Genesis (after 2:4 and 5:1) and marks the beginning of the Noahic narrative unit (6:9–9:29). It is the structural spine of the book.

Three epithets for Noah: (1) אִישׁ צַדִּיק — "a righteous man" (relational-legal righteousness, right standing within community and covenant); (2) תָּמִים — "blameless" (wholeness/integrity; same term used for unblemished sacrificial animals, Lev. 1:3); (3) הִתְהַלֶּךְ אֶת-הָאֱלֹהִים — "walked with God" (Hitpael of הָלַךְ, iterative-habitual: he kept walking; the identical phrase used of Enoch, 5:22, 5:24).

**"In his generations" (בְּדֹרֹתָיו):** Left ambiguous by consensus. Two readings: (a) laudatory — he was blameless in the context of his corrupt time, an extraordinary achievement; (b) limiting — he was blameless relative to his generation, implying a lower bar than absolute righteousness (Rashi's reading). The text does not adjudicate. Neither does the consensus.

### 6:14 — עֲצֵי-גֹפֶר (gopher wood) — hapax legomenon

גֹּפֶר appears only here in the Hebrew Bible. Ancient identifications: the LXX (Vaticanus) reads ξύλα τετράγωνα ("squared timbers" — a processing description, not a species); the Vulgate reads lignis laevigatis ("smoothed timbers"); Targum Onkelos uses qadros (possibly cedar). Modern proposals include cypress, cedar, pine, acacia, laminated wood. None is established. The consensus transliterates.

### 6:16 — צֹהַר (tsohar) — near-hapax

צֹהַר appears only in this verse (as a noun in this sense). The cognate צָהֳרַיִם means "noon / the bright time of day," suggesting a connection to light or brightness. Proposed meanings: a window or opening to admit light; a skylight or translucent covering; a roof or overhang; a ridge opening. The Talmud (Sanhedrin 108b) speaks of a luminous stone placed in the ark — a midrashic tradition that may preserve memory of the original sense. The LXX (Vaticanus): ἐπισυνάγων ποιήσεις — "making it convergent / gathered" — suggesting a tapering roof structure; the Greek is itself obscure. The consensus transliterates: "tsohar."

### 6:17 — מַבּוּל (mabbul) — the technical flood term

מַבּוּל appears only in Genesis 6–9 and Psalm 29:10 (where YHWH "sits enthroned at the mabbul"). It is not the ordinary Hebrew word for a flood (שֶׁטֶף or נָהָר). Its etymology is debated; possible connection to Akkadian nabālu ("destruction"). It is a technical term for this specific primordial cosmic event. In Greek it becomes κατακλυσμόν (kataklusmos) — the term that yields English "cataclysm."

### 6:18 — בְּרִיתִי (beriti) — first covenant in the Bible

This is the first occurrence of the word בְּרִית (berit, "covenant") in the Hebrew Bible. The Hiphil of קוּם (וַהֲקִמֹתִי, "I will establish/cause to stand") indicates the enactment of a divinely determined commitment — not its initiation for the first time but its formal establishment. The covenant is announced here (v. 18) and fully articulated after the flood (9:8–17). It is announced before Noah enters the ark — grace and promise precede the ordeal.

### 6:22 — Compliance formula

"According to all that God commanded him — so he did." The doubled formula (כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה / כֵּן עָשָׂה) is emphatic: total obedience to total command. The formula recurs at the completion of the ark's loading (ch. 7) and structurally parallels the tabernacle completion formulae in Exodus 39–40 (Exod. 39:32, 39:43, 40:16), where Israel's compliance with God's building instructions is similarly doubled and underscored. Noah's obedience in Genesis 6 and Israel's obedience at Sinai share the same narrative grammar.

The divine name in v. 22 is אֱלֹהִים (Elohim), consistent with the pattern from v. 9 onward. The shift from יהוה (the LORD, vv. 1–8) to אֱלֹהִים (God, vv. 9–22) tracks with the structural boundary at the toledot formula: YHWH as the relational-covenantal name in the section of divine grief and grace; Elohim as the universal, ordering name in the section of ark specifications and covenant announcement.

## LXX (Vaticanus) Comparison — Genesis 6

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Consensus Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| 6:2 | בְּנֵי-הָאֱלֹהִים — "sons of God" | υἱοὶ τοῦ θεοῦ — "sons of God" | "sons of God" — MT and Vaticanus agree; other LXX mss read "angels of God" |
| 6:3 | לֹא-יָדוֹן רוּחִי — "shall not strive" | οὐ μὴ καταμείνῃ — "shall not remain" | "shall not strive" — MT tradition adopted; LXX reading noted |
| 6:4 | הַנְּפִלִים — "Nephilim" | οἱ γίγαντες — "the giants" | "the Nephilim" — transliteration; LXX interpretive gloss not adopted |
| 6:6 | וַיִּנָּחֶם — "was grieved" | ἐνεθυμήθη — "pondered/considered" | "was grieved" — MT's anthropopathic language retained; LXX softening not adopted |
| 6:6 | וַיִּתְעַצֵּב אֶל-לִבּוֹ | καὶ διενοήθη — "and he reflected" | "it grieved him to his heart" — MT's directional grief-to-the-heart retained |
| 6:9 | הִתְהַלֶּךְ נֹחַ אֶת-הָאֱלֹהִים | εὐηρέστησεν τῷ θεῷ — "pleased God" | "Noah walked with God" — MT's walking-with metaphor retained |
| 6:14 | עֲצֵי-גֹפֶר — "gopher wood" | ξύλα τετράγωνα — "squared timbers" | "gopher wood" — hapax transliterated; LXX species-guess not adopted |
| 6:16 | צֹהַר — "tsohar" | ἐπισυνάγων — "gathered/convergent" | "tsohar" — hapax transliterated; both MT and LXX are uncertain |
| 6:17 | מַבּוּל — "the flood" | κατακλυσμόν — "kataklusmos" | "the flood of waters" — technical term preserved without transliteration |
| 6:19 | שְׁנַיִם מִכֹּל — "two of every kind" | δύο δύο — "two by two" | "two of every kind" — MT formulation; LXX distributive not adopted |
